coredata.dc:title: Sonochemical synthesis of carbon dots, mechanism, effect of parameters, and catalytic, energy, biomedical and tissue engineering applications
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Raj
Kumar, Vijay Bhooshan
Gedanken, Aharon
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based nanomaterials are gaining more and more interest because of their wide range of applications. Carbon dots (CDs) have shown exclusive interest due to unique and novel physicochemical, optical, electrical, and biological properties. Since their discovery, CDs became a promising material for wide range of research applications from energy to biomedical and tissue engineering applications. At same time several new methods have been developed for the synthesis of CDs. Compared to many of these methods, the sonochemical preparation is a green method with advantages such as facile, mild experimental conditions, green energy sources, and feasibility to formulate CDs and doped CDs with controlled physicochemical properties and lower toxicity. In the last five years, the sonochemically synthesized CDs were extensively studied in a wide range of applications. In this review, we discussed the sonochemical assisted synthesis of CDs, doped CDs and their nanocomposites. In addition to the synthetic route, we will discuss the effect of various experimental parameters on the physicochemical properties of CDs; and their applications in different research areas such as bioimaging, drug delivery, catalysis, antibacterial, polymerization, neural tissue engineering, dye absorption, ointments, electronic devices, lithium ion batteries, and supercapacitors. This review concludes with further research directions to be explored for the applications of sonochemical synthesized CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based nanomaterials are gaining more and more interest because of their wide range of applications. Carbon dots (CDs) have shown exclusive interest due to unique and novel physicochemical, optical, electrical, and biological properties. Since their discovery, CDs became a promising material for wide range of research applications from energy to biomedical and tissue engineering applications. At same time several new methods have been developed for the synthesis of CDs. Compared to many of these methods, the sonochemical preparation is a green method with advantages such as facile, mild experimental conditions, green energy sources, and feasibility to formulate CDs and doped CDs with controlled physicochemical properties and lower toxicity. In the last five years, the sonochemically synthesized CDs were extensively studied in a wide range of applications. In this review, we discussed the sonochemical assisted synthesis of CDs, doped CDs and their nanocomposites. In addition to the synthetic route, we will discuss the effect of various experimental parameters on the physicochemical properties of CDs; and their applications in different research areas such as bioimaging, drug delivery, catalysis, antibacterial, polymerization, neural tissue engineering, dye absorption, ointments, electronic devices, lithium ion batteries, and supercapacitors. This review concludes with further research directions to be explored for the applications of sonochemical synthesized CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials are versatile materials with diverse physical, chemical, optical, electrical, magnetic, and electrochemical properties. Nanomaterials are broadly classified as organic and inorganic. Since the discovery of carbon allotropes such as fullerenes [1], carbon nanotubes [2], and graphene [3], that have attracted significant attention due to their exclusive properties and impact on the range of applications. CDs are known to be one of the best types of fluorescent nanomaterial, owing to their superior solubility in water [4], excellent biocompatibility [5], optical property [6], chemical inertness [7], neglectable cytotoxicity [8], and capacity for functionalization with different chemical entities. Therefore, highly fluorescent CDs have acquired increasing research attention in the last few years [9–11]. CDs are quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterials, which have less than 10 nm size in all three dimensions [12]. The expansion of publications on CDs has clearly been seen in increasing the number of literature reports and citations year by year. The first report on the CDs appeared in 2004, as a new type of carbon nanomaterials, the fourth allotrope of carbon, and was named CDs [4]. CD is a more general term used for various nanoscale carbon materials such as graphene quantum dots, polymer quantum dots, and carbon quantum dots or carbon dots. Broadly, materials at nanoscale with major composition of carbon are called carbon dots [13]. The major property of carbon dots is its fluorescence. CDs consist of sp2 carbon they can be considered as composed of 2–3 small parallel grapheme sheets. CDs contain oxygen, hydrogen, and carbon in substantial quantities located on their surface. Their main advantages are the excellent optical properties, superior biocompatibility, ultra-small particle size, easy and simple preparation feasibility, and the low cost of their synthesis make them first choice of priority to researchers. CDs have shown a potential use in various research fields such as bioimaging, cell imaging, dye degradation, chemiluminescence, solar cells, catalysis, drug delivery, polymers, tissue engineering, photocatalysts, nanoelectronics’ devices, photodynamic therapy, and gene delivery [14–18].

There are number of methods for the synthesis of CDs such as laser ablation, combustion, pyrolysis, hydrothermal method, precipitation, and the sonochemical method [15,19,20]. Each method has its own advantages and limitations. For example, chemical based synthesis frequently involved toxic chemicals which induced cytotoxicity to CDs, and hence limited their use in biological applications. Most of the methods, such as precipitation process produce very good quality CDs, but the product yield% was very low with low solid loading, difficulties in the removal of large volume of solvents, and formation of amorphous nanoparticles are the major limitations for scale up production and industrial applications. The development of new and innovative synthesis techniques remains a challenge. Compared to other techniques, the sonochemical method is a green process [21,22]. Large number of researchers including us have been working on developing new kind of nanomaterials using sonochemistry [23–25]. Since 2013, we have been working on the synthesis and applications of CDs. In addition to the synthesis of CDs we have also been involved with the sonochemical preparation of CDs-based nanomaterials (metal and nonmetal doped CDs, CDs conjugated nanomaterials) and employed them in range of applications such as ointments [26], antibacterial [27], lubricants [28], lithium ion batteries [29], supercapacitors [30], superconductivity [31], cell labeling [32], catalysis [33], polymerization [34], biofuel [35,36], solar cells [37], drug delivery, and neural tissue engineering [38–44]. We have published more than 30 manuscripts on sonochemistry and CDs properties. To the best of our knowledge, there is no review articles published so far with a major focus on the combination of sonochemistry and CDs. Here, for the first time we reviewed the sonochemical synthesis of CDs, and doped CDs, the effect of sonication parameters on their physicochemical properties and a range of their applications.

The techniques for the synthesis of CDs can be broadly classified into two categories based on approaches such as top-down and bottom-up methods [45,46]. Top-down methods are laser ablation, arc discharge, electrochemical processes, and plasma assisted synthesis [47]. The bottom-up methods include the pyrolytic method, template assisted synthesis, microwave assisted synthesis, chemical processes, reverse micelle technique, and the sonochemical method [48–50]. Various synthesis techniques, precursors used for CDs formulation, quantum yield, and emission properties are listed in Table 1 .

The sonochemical synthesis process has numerous important applications in organic synthesis, materials, organometallic chemistry, and industrial manufacturing processes [76–80]. Standard sonochemical process for carbon dots synthesis is as follows: in brief, carbon precursors with known concentration in a suitable solvent are subjected to sonication for desired time. The radiation energy utilized for condensation and carbonization results for the formation of CDs. Sonochemical assisted synthesis of CDs was attempted by Li et al. using alkali or acid assisted ultrasonic treatment of glucose as a precursor material followed by ultrasonic irradiation [65]. Then Wei et al. developed facile sonochemical method using pyrolysis of carbon precursors (citric acid and ethylene diamine) inside implosively collapsing bubbles, which make nitrogen (N) doped CDs (N@CDs) [81]. However, these processes are multi step, catalysts are used, low yield, and poor quantum yield of the CDs is achieved. Recently, Kumar et al. have for the first-time successfully formulated the CDs synthesis in a single step using ultrasound radiation without a catalyst and a post acid/base treatment [82]. Briefly, 12 mL of polyethylene glycol (PEG 400) is placed in a test tube which is dipped in water/silicon oil bath, then an ultrasonic transducer is immersed up to 1 cm above the bottom of the tube and subjected to ultrasound irradiation. Fig. 1 is presenting the simple experimental set up of sonochemical synthesis of CDs. The Figure demonstrates also the preparation of tin doped CDs (Sn@CDs) (Fig. 1a) and carbon dot decorated substrates (Fig. 1b). It is worth mentioning that the metal doping of the CDs was performed from the metal in their liquid state. Unlike the preparation of CDs where many different synthetic routes are reported, the sonochemcial preparation of metal@CDs is unique. It applies to metals having a low melting point such as Ga, In, Bi, Pb, Sn, Sb, Cd, and Zn. For all these metals the sonication is carried out in a solvent overlayering the liquid metal. There is no other general technique for the synthesis of Metal@CDs. This is an exclusive for the sonochemical procedure.

Kumar et al. thoroughly investigated the effect of different experimental parameters such as temperature (4–150 °C), sonication time (3 min, 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 2.5 h and 3 h) and ultrasound amplitude (40–70%) [82] on the sonochemical synthesis of CDs.

Results showed that the CDs yield is increased with the time of sonication, power of sonication, and temperature. The typical size of the formed CDs is ranged from 2 to 7 nm. The dots illustrated in Fig. 2 showed ~16% quantum yield [82]. Currently, there are several more reports available in literature on the preparation of CDs using sonochemistry [84].

Like organic nanoparticles, the bare CDs are less stable, show a low fluorescence and poor quantum yield; hence it remains a challenge to prepare highly fluorescence CDs with good colloidal stability and high quantum yield. To prepare the highly stabilized CDs with controlled particles size, different stabilizers can be used. However, quantum yield can’t be improved when stabilizers are used. Various investigations towards enhancing quantum yields have been done and the results revealed that doping with metals or other elements could be a one of the best ways to improve the quantum yield of CDs [85]. To prepare the doped CDs, suitable precursor of doping element or metal can used in suitable concentration. There are several materials have been used as doping agent such as metals (gold, silver, gallium, tin, and lead) and elements (nitrogen, phosphorous, Sulphur, and boron), and promising results were observed [32,83]. One of the widely reported doping agents for carbon dot is nitrogen. This is due to large number of nitrogen’s doping precursors such as amino acids and proteins that are available. Secondly, the doping of nitrogen is easy and simple compared to other compounds due to close atomic, molecular, crystal, and physicochemical properties of nitrogen and carbon which makes nitrogen doping favorable [67,85].

Kumar et al. has fabricated a range of doped CDs with metals such as gold, silver, gallium, tin, lead, through one-pot sonochemical synthesis process without any stabilizer and any catalyst [83]. Moreover, preparation of doped carbon dots depends on the selection of proper precursors for carbon dots, and the doping sources. [86,87]. However, doping only affect limited optical properties of carbon dots, whereas, sonication parameters affect physicochemical properties of carbon dots. Hence, it is more interesting to understand the effect of sonication parameters on carbon dot synthesis process. In the following section, we discuss the effect of sonochemical reaction parameters on the synthesis of carbon dots.

CDs have range of attractive properties such as sensitivity, high chemical stability, conductivity, optical transparency, high mechanical strength, narrow bandgap, high environmental friendliness, and strong photoluminescence. Moreover, they are biodegradable, biocompatible, non-toxic, have good colloidal stability, superior photostability and multicolor emission, and broad excitation spectra [34,88,89]. The most interesting property of carbon dots is fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity can be expressed by the quantum yield. Quantum yield (QY) is the ratio between the number of photons emitted and the number of photons absorbed by the CDs [67,90,91]. There are several methods to calculate the QY. The most reliable method for recording QY is the comparative method, which involves the use of well characterized standard samples with known QY values [91–94]. Recently, Kumar et al. has used the very simple method to calculate the QY of fluorescence CDs. Briefly, the CDs fluorescence was estimated by comparing the integrated PL (Photo Luminescence) intensities and the absorbance values at the same wavelength with that of the quinine sulfate in 0.2 M H2SO4 (quantum yield = 54%). Specifically, the absorbance values of CDs or doped CDs and quinine sulfate were measured at 360 nm [82]. The decay times of the various CDs were also measured and found to be in the range of 2–4 ns (ns).

Table 2 presents the calculated QY with several other physical, chemical and biological properties of sonochemically synthesized CDs and doped CDs. Moreover, the diversity of the structures, CDs possess some similar optical properties in terms of their absorption and fluorescence. Herein, we have summarized the common optical properties rather than consider specific examples [95].

In addition to that, more drastic competition between different relaxation channels created by irregular carbonization or surface states via sonication, which are out of control. To the best of our knowledge, these carbonized CDs and surface states with different metal/nonmetal doping via sonication are still disordered and mainly dependent on synthesis parameter, source of carbon and post-functionalization. The effect of other experimental parameters is widely discussed in the literature [21,22]. However, we discuss the effect of sonication parameter in following sections.

CDs physicochemical properties are highly dependent on the particle size and shape; hence it is remained a challenge to synthesize the CDs with controlled size and shape for desired applications. The chemical reaction is mainly depending on the energy required to complete the reaction and to form a product. Similarly, for the CDs synthesis minimum energy is needed, in sonochemical process this energy is generated from cavitation [82]. There are several parameters that affect the CDs synthesis they include the solvent, ultrasound frequency, power of ultrasound, sonication amplitude, temperature of reaction, sonication time, precursors, catalyst, and doping material and their concentrations. In following sections, we briefly discuss the effect of major reaction parameters in the sonochemical synthesis of CDs [80].

The acoustic cavitation phenomena are generated in solution/liquid/solvent through ultrasound radiation. Hence, properties of solvent such as viscosity, boiling point, surface tension, and vapor pressures are the critical parameters affecting the acoustic cavitation, and hence the particle size and shape of the CDs [104]. Solvent with lower viscosity and surface tension favor the growth of bubble, which helps the formation of good size particles with more stability [105]. Wang et al. fabricated fluorine doped carbon nitride quantum dots using sonochemical process using different solvents such as water, ethanol (EN), 1,3-butanediol, and ethylene glycol (EG). Large nanosheets, small nanosheets of CDs are obtained in case of water and ethanol, respectively. Uniform sized nanoparticles (1–2 nm) of CDs were obtained when the solvent was 1,3-butanediol and ethylene glycol [106]. This is due to the velocity of mass transfer is high in low viscosity solvents (ethanol, water) compared to high viscosity solvents (ethylene glycol, 1,3-butanediol). Further advantages of ethylene glycol and 1,3-butanediol are: being a better radical acceptance and reduction capability of solvent, compared to EN and H2O [101]

Solute addition decreases surface tension, with the increase in the solute concentration, further decrease of surface tension results in the formation of more stable CDs. It is also widely reported that the use of surfactant enhance the stability of CDs or nanoparticles [107]. This is due to the decrease in the surface tension resulting from the presence of surfactant in solvent, and hence more stable nanoparticles are formed. Moreover, stabilizers absorbed on the surface help to stabilize the particle and control the growth of particles [22,38,42]. When the CDs are prepared sonochemically from the PEG-400 precursor their formation is followed by the condensation, dehydration, and dehydrogenation [32,83,87]. It is also reported that the preparation of 2D and 3D materials require longer sonication time as compared to the fabrication of spherical shaped nanoparticles (1D) and CDs (0D) [78,108]. Longer time of sonication extend the growth of materials. The broad range of solvent has not been explored for the synthesis of CDs and metal doped CDs, which will be studied in the future.

The sonication frequency directly affects the growth or collapse of bubbles; higher frequency favors the collapse of the bubbles. Whereas, lower frequency allows the growth of the bubbles to larger sizes [78,109]. Higher frequencies can help to restrict the growth of bubble and results in the formation of smaller size bubble, and hence smaller size particles are formed [110]. However, there is no direct relationship between size of the bubble, energy inside the bubble, and frequency. The frequency also affects the cavitation, however, optimum frequency generates smaller size nanoparticles, whereas above and below the optimum frequency generates larger size particles [110–113]. The widely acceptable frequency range is 100–400 kHz for the synthesis of nanoparticles or nanomaterials including CDs with smaller size. Moreover, there are no reports on the ultrasonic frequency effect on the formation of CDs, this will another topic which can be explore in the future. All sonochemical studies reported in literature were carried out at a frequency of 20 kHz.

The sonication power is an important parameter in determining the size and shape of the CDs. Higher power will disturb the cavitation, and hence uncontrolled growth of bubbles will takes place [110]. This affects the formation of nanoparticles with broader particle size distribution [114]. Similar to frequency, power also need to take care and used at best levels to generate the nanoparticles with uniform size and shape [105,110]. The use of 20–30 W power is suitable for preparation of range of nanoparticles as per best of our experience for preparation of nanoparticles of metallic, CDs and doped CDs.

Time of sonication is also affect the size of the CDs and doped CDs. Furthermore, long-time sonication (more than 6 h) has resulted in the heterogenous nanoparticles [115–117]. This is due to that the initially formed nanoparticles act as seeds and help the formation of larger size particles. The deposition of smaller size particles on the surface of preformed CDs will yield large size particles depending on the concentration of precursor or smaller size particles [29,43,118,119]. Whereas for preparation of CDs, 90–180 min of sonication are required. Recently, Kumar et al. performed several experiments at various sonication durations of 1, 2, 2.5 and 3 h, at 70% amplitude and starting temperatures of 55 °C [93]. The intensity of the fluorescence of the CDs is time dependent, and the most intense fluorescent was obtained for 3 h sonication. Sonication for only 30 min yielded very low fluorescence [93]. It can thus be concluded that the decomposition of PEG to form C-dots is time dependent, with a certain threshold for each set of conditions.

For the synthesis of carbon dots a variety of precursors have been reported. The most widely used precursors for the synthesis of CDs are classified into three categories 1) natural sources, 2) molecular compounds, and 3) small organic compounds. Natural sources are orange juice, banana juice, beer, egg, potato, meat, beverage, coffee, soya milk, punica granatum fruit, sugar, bread, jiggery, lysozyme, sucrose, starch, grass, and bombyxmori silk [120,121] are highly used as precursors. The molecular compounds used for the CDs synthesis are ammonium citrate, octadecylammonium citrate, and sodium 11-aminoundecanoate are the major precursors [85]. Small organic compounds were also used as precursors they include citric acid, boric acid, benzene, amino acids, bovine serum albumin (BSA) proteins, polyethylene glycol, glycerol, glucose, ethylene glycol, and N-acetylcysteine, diamine have been widely investigated [36,43,82]. However, the use of precursors depends on the method of fabrication of CDs. The different precursors have given different physicochemical properties to the CDs. For example, PEG-400 is considered the best precursor for the preparation of CDs [83]. Compared to other precursors, small organic compounds are suitable to breakage of chemical bonds forming CDs. Hence, small organic compounds result in higher yields of CDs. Large size chemical entities need more energy to complete conversion into CDs. Hence, lower yields of CDs are obtained at identical experimental conditions. For nitrogen doping into CDs, proteins/amino acids are well studied compounds which easily formed nitrogen doped CDs. Similarly, boric acid [(B(OH)3] is good choice for boron doping CDs (B@CDs) fabricated via the sonochemical process [32]. Another advantage of boric acid is it contains not only boron but also other elements such as hydrogen and oxygen. That is the reason it produces B@CDs without any byproduct. The proper concentration of boric acid generates efficient doped CDs, at higher concentrations it might result by-products or composite materials instead of the desired B doped CDs products. There are several catalysts that can be used in the synthesis of CDs. The major problem is that they might remain inside the CDs, because of the difficulty to separate them. Catalysts can also induce cytotoxicity into CDs. Hence, it is widely suggested to avoid the use of catalyst in CDs synthesis in the sonochemical process. Most of the catalysts are not suitable for sonochemical process due to inability to survive the high energy released in the sonication process. From the above discussion and existing literature, and as per the best of our knowledge, the effect of different sonication parameters on carbon dots is not yet well established. Further investigations are suggested to be conducted to understand these sonication parameters and their influence on the formation of CDs.
Due to the wide range of unique and novel properties, CDs are excellent materials for diverse applications. We discuss the application of CDs and doped CDs in following sections.

Antibacterial resistance of many bacteria against available antibiotics have been steadily increasing. Development of new antibiotics have been in demand, because of the limited availability of antibiotics. One of the promising alternatives is material science and nanotechnology. Materials at nanoscales showed enhanced antibacterial properties [122]. There are a number of studies reported on the antibacterial activity of nanomaterials such as metals (silver, iron, gallium) [27,123–126], carbon-based materials, and metal oxides (ZnO, TiO2) [126,127]. Among carbon-based materials, CDs are also one of the more interesting materials due to their small particle size, fluorescence properties, higher cellular uptake, water solubility, biocompatibility, and biodegradability [123]. Recently, Kumar et al. observed interesting antibacterial performance against P. aeruginosa for carbon dots doped with Ga nanoparticles fabricated sonochemically [27]. This is due to combined effect of antibacterial property of carbon dots and gallium nanoparticles. The sonochemically prepared Ga doped CDs (Ga@CDs) showed excellent stability over 2 months and antibacterial studies revealed that doping of gallium in the range between 70 and 340 µg into 1 g of CDs showed MIC values of 0.34 to 1.36 ppm [27].

Parasitic diseases are most common around the world. According to World Health Organization (WHO), 14 million people are infected with skin related diseases and 2 million new cases are reported annually [128]. The treatment time for skin diseases are too long. However, diagnostic tests for skin diseases are very limited. Moreover, they are not very sensitive, selective, and time consuming. Hence, reliable and sensitive procedure with cost-effective early detection need to be developed [129,130]. Leishmaniasis, is a vector-borne human disease caused by obligate intracellular protozoa of the genus Leishmania via transmission by sand flies [131]. Due to low cost of preparation, superior optical properties, and excellent biocompatibility of sonochemically synthesized CDs, they are good choice of materials for the development of treatment for skin related diseases. Kumar et al. explored this additional application of the Ga@CDs synthesized sonochemically. They investigated the application of CDs and Ga doped CDs formulating a fluorescent CDs-based ointment. They were first to report on an optically active CD-based ointment, to the best of our knowledge, for treating leishmania donovani, without any side effects and good solubility [26]. Due to limitation of optical properties of existing ointment, it is difficult to track the ointment in cells and tissues in-vivo. The ointment with the optical fluorescent property of the incorporated CDs (4–8 nm) enabled tracking the CDs in the parasitic cells. The optically active detectable ointment showed effectiveness against the parasites without harm to host cells (Fig. 4 ). Fig. 4 presents the parasitic activity against L. major (Fig. 4a) and L. donovani (Fig. 4b). Fig. 4c and 4d showed the interaction of CDs with ointment and mechanism, respectively. This is due to penetration of CDs into the cell membrane and interaction with other biomolecules and organelles [26]. Hence, this study opens a new window for the development of detectable ointment and more studies are needed to investigate the efficacy of CDs and doped CDs against other parasites and skin related diseases treatments even with control targeting feasibility. The CDs can be incorporated into wide variety of products such as drug delivery patches to track drug delivery [26]. Through remotely controlling the CDs, precise control and targeted delivery of drug can be realistic for patients with dementia.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug delivery, biomedical and neural tissue engineering
ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of carbon based nanomaterials, one of the widely studied application of carbon nanotubes, graphene, and fullerenes is drug and gene delivery [132,133]. This is due to the feasibility of carbon nanomaterials and the various types of interaction such as pi-pi stacking, hydrophobic, and electrostatic attraction favor the loading of a range of drug molecules. The large surface area of these carbon entities is facilitating excellent encapsulation, and drug loading efficiency. Along with the above-mentioned properties, CDs are also been employed for drug delivery. This is because of their biocompatibility, low relative cytotoxicity, and good cell uptake which makes them excellent materials for drug and gene delivery [134,135]. Due to their fluorescence properties, the delivery of the drug, interaction with cells, tissues, can be monitored through bioimaging techniques, which help to understand the in vivo challenges associated with drug delivery and further help in better formulation for drug delivery [136]. CDs are effective, safe, and visible nanocarrier systems. Sonochemical assisted synthesis of CDs and doped CDs showed excellent biocompatibility [83]. PEI functionalized CDs were used for delivery of DNA by Liu et al [137]. CDs conjugated with metal nanoparticles were used as nanocarriers for control and targeting drug and gene delivery applications [69,133].

Regeneration of damaged nerve is one of the big challenges in neural diseases. However, nerves have regeneration capacity, but it is very slow and limited [139]. There are several approaches that have been developed to enhance regeneration, one such process is the use of scaffold materials or biomaterials to the delivery of nerve growth factor with controlled manner, locally, at the site of action [140,141]. There is high demand of biocompatible nanomaterials for neural tissue engineering applications since most of the nanomaterials are toxic, and hence limited their use in applications. At the same time most of the nanomaterials are neglected or not investigated for neuroengineering applications. Recently, Kumar et al. [83] employed the use of CDs based nanomaterials for neural tissue engineering. They conducted extensive studies of various types of CDs formulated through sonochemistry. The studies included biocompatibility with neuronal cells, effect of CDs on the differentiation process of neural cell, and outgrowth of neural network. It was also reported that all CDs formulated through sonochemical process showed excellent biocompatibility even at low concentration of CDs (0.1 mg/mL) and didn’t affect the neuronal cells differentiation processes [83]. It is concluded that sonochemically synthesized CDs are more promising materials for biomedical and tissue engineering application due to very low cytotoxicity compared to chemical based formulations [32].

To improve the quantum yield of CDs, Kumar et al. prepared for the first time, CDs doped with different metals and elements. They successfully doped the CDs with five different metals such as gold, silver, tin, zinc, gallium through sonication process. All prepared doped CDs showed excellent biocompatibility against neuronal cells even at concentration 0.1 mg/mL. This behavior was demonstrated against PC12 cells [83]. Fig. 5 presents the confocal images of CDs and M@CDs treated with neuronal cells SH-SY5Y. The M@CDs didn’t affect the neuronal cells (PC12 cells) differentiation processes. Nissan et al. fabricated the Ga@C-dots@Ga nanoparticles using sonochemical irradiation of PEG 400 and molten Ga with particle size ranging from 50 to 200 nm [43]. The prepared Ga@C-dots@Ga coated glass substrate showed excellent supporting material for neuronal cells differentiation and outgrowth compare to bare glass substrate. Results revealed that 97% enhancement in the number of branches initiated from the soma in neural growth [43]. This is due to favorable interaction between the nanoparticles on the surface and neuronal cells. It is also widely reported that topographic cues [142], and nano-pattern substrates [143], and aligned polymer fibers [144] affect the neural cells differentiation processes. This interesting study opens a new window for CDs serving for neural tissue engineering application. Further studies are needed to understand the physicochemical interactions of the CDs with biomolecules and biomaterials.

CDs contain carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen in substantial quantities. These fascinating structures make them attractive in catalytic applications [145]. CDs and functionalized CDs can be used as catalyst in the polymerization reaction, which are initiated through free radicals [146]. Sun et al. reported the electron donors and acceptor behavior of CDs under photoexcitation [147]. Kang et al. utilized this photoenhanced hydrogen-bond catalytic activity of CDs in the Aldol condensation reaction for used as organic transformation [148]. CDs are utilized by Zhao et al. in free radical polymerization, where CDs act as free radical scavengers [149]. This helped to yield the higher molecular weight polymers. Gedanken and his colleagues also investigated the catalytic application of sonochemical synthesized CDs in photocatalysis and polymerization reaction [88,146]. The nanocomposites of carbon dots also showed excellent catalytic performance [33]. Kumar et al. formulated the Sn@C-dots/TiO2 nanocomposites through a two-step sonochemical process [33]. Molten tin and PEG at 260 °C were subjected to sonication to form the Sn@C-dots. Further sonication was applied by the addition of TiO2, which facilitated the adhesion of Sn@C-dots on TiO2 surface. The fabricated nanocomposite tested for their photocatalytic performance under solar radiation. Better results were in the photodegradation of methylene blue and crystal violet under solar light than TiO2 and C-dot/TiO2 [33]. Fig. 7 presents the mechanism of photocatalysis, charge separation, and photodegradation presence of Sn@C-dot/TiO2 as photocatalyst under sunlight.

Polymers are formed through polymerization of monomers. The Polymerization process can be done by using different initiators or catalysts. There are different types of polymerization processes based on the different mechanisms of polymerization [150]. One such type is radical polymerization, where the free radicals act as initiator and initiate the synthesis of polymers [151]. Among various types of polymers, conducting polymers are more interesting because they are involved in various applications including supercapacitors, electronic devices, sensors, and optical devices [152–157]. The synthesis of conducting polymers such as polyaniline (PANI) and polypyrrole (PPY) requires strong oxidizing agents for their preparation. Chemical oxidization processes are widely used to polymerize molecules such as aniline (monomer). Initiators play key role in polymerizations, for example, potassium persulfate and ammonium persulfate which are used in the polymerization of aniline and pyrrole. However, the disadvantage of their use as initiators, is their toxicity, side effects, chemical hazardous, and safety is the matter of concern [153,154]. Recently, an interesting property of CDs was found, it was reported that CDs are generating free radical in aqueous solution. Hence, CDs are promising materials that can be used as initiator in polymerization reactions [158]. Moorthy et al. thoroughly investigated the use of CDs prepared sonichemically, and employed them as initiators for and successful polymerization of several polymers including aniline and pyrrole [146]. Micro and nanoparticles of polymers of PANI (polyaniline) and PPY (polypyrrole) were formulated using one-pot polymerization reaction in the presence of CDs, as catalyst (initiator) [34]. The CDs were used with and without UV light for the polymerization process [159]. To investigate the efficiency of CDs, Moorthy et al. studied the kinetics of methylene blue absorption by PANI and PPY [160]. Fig. 8 a and 8b presents the SEM images of fabricated PANI and PPY using CDs. Absorption kinetics showed peudo-first order and second-order Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms, respectively, for PANI and PPY. PANI and PPY absorption capacities are 19.2 and 19.31 mg/g, respectively. [89]. Recently, it was reported that carbon dots were successfully used in the synthesis of poly-(4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine) (POBBA) and copolymers composed of POBBA [159]. Unlike the synthesis of PANI and PPY that required the CDs and UV light, for the synthesis of POBBA only the CDs were enough without UV light. Moreover, the preparation of POBBA was never reported before. Fig. 8c and 8d presents the SEM images of POBBA and copolymers synthesized using sonochemically prepared CDs. Results revealed that the kinetics of polymerization depends on reaction temperature, reaction time, and efficiency of carbon dots as initiators [159]. Interestingly, the formed polymers were generated as particles with size ranging from 262 nm to 2.5 µm under different experimental conditions. Hence, it was concluded that, carbon dots can be a promising material for the synthesis of polymeric micro-nanoparticles directly. We used the sonochemical synthesized CDs as initiators for the polymerization of 4,4′-diaminodiphenylmethane (PDDM) monomer. Moreover, the prepared polymer PDDM (poly(4,4′-diaminodiphenyl-methane)) showed excellent adsorption of methylene blue with the efficacy of its removal reached 92% in 120 min [88].

The progress of new application of nanomaterials is growing rapidly. In the last 10 years, the search for new batteries has grown as a specialized research area. Due to the rapid growth of human population consumption of energy has been increased. Conventional energy sources such as oil and natural gas are predicted to be depleted in the future, hence energy generation and storage demand is growing consistently around the world [161–163]. Traditional energy storage devices are capacitors and batteries which can be replaced with rechargeable batteries. The advantages of these devices are: good safety, good energy density, and performance [161]. Initially, graphite was used as anode, but recent investigation of several nanomaterials such as carbon based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes, nitrogen doped graphene sheets, and doped carbon nanotubes showed promising results [164]. The feasibility to tune the physicochemical properties of carbon dots doped with various materials, makes them interesting materials for superior electrochemical performance in rechargeable batteries [165,166]. Kumar et al. formulated the Sn@c-dot@Sn (Sn nanoparticles decorated with Sn doped carbon dots) nanoparticles through a single step technique using the sonochemical method. They used polyethylene glycol (PEG) as precursor. The prepared nanoparticles deposited electrodes showed promising performance as an anode in lithium ion batteries [29]. Fig. 9 a, 9b, and 9c are presenting the results of cyclic voltammetry, charge and discharge profile, and rate capability studies, respectively [29].

Superconductivity is a phenomenon in which the electrical resistivity suddenly drops to zero at its transition temperature Tc. It is one of the hot topics in the field of research for decades. The superconductivity is one the challenging scientific problems, which will impact the energy storage and transportation with zero resistance at room temperature [167,168]. As it is well known that the physicochemical properties of nanomaterials differ from the same bulk counterpart. Hence, number of researchers investigated the superconductive behavior of nanomaterials [169]. The agglomeration and oxidation of the nanomaterials is a major issue in superconducting behavior of nanomaterials. One of the promising solutions to overcome this drawback is coating of carbon on the surface of nanomaterials [170]. Shani et al. fabricated the carbon layer coated with tin nanospheres ranging from 120 nm to 1400 nm. The coating was performed sonochemically. Measurements of the magnetic moment of the prepared product showed type-1 superconductivity with critical field (Hc), like bulk tin. The effective coherence length reduced below the size of the nanospheres of carbon coated tin [31]. However, superconductivity at room temperature remains a challenge. Further, Shani et al. fabricated the carbon coated Sn and Pb spheres through sonication. Among the formed samples, carbon coated Pb nano-sphere showed enhanced critical field Hc. In contrast, Sn didn’t show such an enhancement. This is due to change in their effective coherence length to below the size of the nanospheres [31].

Kumar et al. attempted to prepare the composite of CDs with modified activated carbon. The CDs are prepared by sonochemical method using PEG 400. The prepared CDs were used for the modification of matrices of activated carbon. The deposition of the CDs on the activated carbon was also done sonochemically. The composite is comprised of pores of activated carbon incorporated by CDs. The formulated composite was used as electrode materials for supercapacitors. The fabricated composites showed an impact on the excellent performance of the electrode [30]. The energy efficacy was found to be >96% than pristine AC, with a capacitance value of 134 F/g, which is much higher than pristine AC (100 F/g) (Fig. 9d and e). The specific surface area is close to double that of the AC.

Due to depletion of fossil fuels and growing populations the demand of renewable energy sources is growing very fast. [171]. The transportation requirement of petroleum is around 30% of the total energy consumption around the world [172]. One of highly accepted renewable energy is biodiesel. Biodiesel can be produced using biological sources such as waste cooking oil and algal oils [173,174]. Microalgae can convert sunlight, CO2, wastewater into fatty acids and triglycerols, which are molecules with high energy storage [175,176]. Compared to homogeneous catalyst, heterogeneous catalyst is more interesting due to advantages associated with it, such as separation and recyclability. The most widely used catalysts for the transesterification process by which biodiesel is synthesized, are NaOH and H2SO4. Among the solid catalysts for the transesterification is SrO. It is one of the best alkali earth metal oxides used for conversion of waste cooking oil into fatty acid methyl esters (FAME). A complete conversion of waste cooking oil to biodiesel with SrO as a catalyst in a microwave domestic oven is achieved in just 10 s [177]. It is also reported that CDs can be used as catalyst for biodiesel [178]. Tangy et al. fabricated the SrO nanoparticles and functionalized it with CDs. He has investigated the catalytic performance of the composite for FAME production from microalgae [179]. The composite was prepared using ultrasonic waves, Sr(NO3)2, and PEG-400 followed by calcination for 4 h at 650 °C. The prepared catalyst showed 97% of lipid conversion value and 45.5% of FAME yield within less than 3 min [179]. Compared to conventional SrO catalyst, it showed 2.4-fold enhancement of the performance. This is due to availability of functional groups such as oxygen, carboxylic, and phenolic on CDs surface, which promotes the esterification of fatty acids [179].

It is clear that sonochemically synthesized carbon dots have shown promising results in various energy applications from batteries to biofuel. However, sonochemical synthesized carbon dots need to be explore further for other energy applications such as water splitting and artificial photosynthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Refractive-Index tuning of highly fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Kumar et al. synthesized nanocomposites of PVA with carbon dot and nitrogen doped CDs using BSA through the sonication method. Patterned thin films were fabricated using femtosecond laser. Schematic setup is shown in Fig. 10 a. The formed nanocomposites with various levels of nitrogen doping showed refractive index (RI) modulation. This study suggested that using N@CDs, RI of PVA can be tunable, the increase of CDs concentration embedded in PVA, has led to increased RI. Fig. 10b presents the variation of the refractive index as a function of concentration of N@CDs in PVA. This can be used to study the RI and to tune it [12].

Tomala et al. synthesized CDs and gallium doped CDs (Ga@CDs) using the sonochemistry of PEG-400, and metallic Ga. In addition, nitrogen doped CDs (N@CDs) was synthesized through a hydrothermal method using BSA. The prepared CDs showed excellent tribological performance as potential lubricants. Doping further with metal and nonmetal enhanced the anti-wear measured using SRV linear oscillation test method. Extreme-pressure performances measured using T-02 Four-Ball method were observed (Fig. 10c and d) [28].

In this review, we discussed the sonochemical synthesis of CDs and doped CDs and the effect of different experimental parameter on the products and their shape and size. First it should be noted that sonochemistry is the only general method for doping metallic particles in CDs. This method can be applied to low melting point metals such as Ga, In, Bi, Sn, Pb, Cd, Sb, and Zn. It is concluded that compared to other techniques the sonochemical synthesized CDs are non-toxic and are excellent materials for biomedical applications due to their biocompatibility. The sonochemical process can prepare the CDs in a single step without the use of a catalyst. Optimization of sonication parameters such as frequency, power, and sonication time are needed to prepare the CDs with desired properties. The fluorescence properties and quantum yield of CDs can be enhanced through doping with metal and other elements. CDs are promising materials in a range of applications such as ointment, antibacterial, bioimaging, cell labeling, drug delivery, catalysts, polymerization, dye degradation, superconducting devices, supercapacitors, and lithium ion batteries due to their excellent physical, chemical, optical, mechanical, conducting, electrical, and fluorescence properties. CDs have been successfully applied for various applications. Most of the studies are focused on well-known applications such as antimicrobial, drug delivery, catalysis, supercapacitors, and batteries. It is important to investigate the use of CDs for novel and unique applications such as wearable electronics, optical active textiles, materials for space applications, and optical switchable drug delivery devices.
